Re
Feln PorrrrcAt. PnacrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA g5gl4-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
March 11,2003
H. Peter Klein, County Counsel
Office of the County Counsel
501 Low Gap Road, Room 1030
Ukiah, CA95482
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-03-006
Dear Mr. Klein:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Richard
Shoemaker, Supervisor for the Second Diskict of the Mendocino County Board of
Supervi.sors, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
'Act').1
QUESTIONS
l. 
Does Supervisor Shoemaker have a conflict of interest disqualifring him
from making,particrpating in making or influencing a vote on the Ukiah Valley Area
Plan ("UVAP'), based on his economic interest in his personal residence and the
improvements to the access road to his residence that would accompany approval ofthe
UVAP?
2. 
Does Supervisor Shoemaker have a conflict of interest disqualifring him
from making, particrpating in making or influencing a vote on the UVAP, based o, his
economic interest in a client who is a source of income to him in excess of $500 over the
past 12 months, and who has an option to purchase properfy within the UVAP area and
seeks rezoning of that property from agricultural to residential for the building of low-
incorne housing, or from the portion of the TIVAP dealing with that decision, if that
decision is segregated from the rest of the UVAP?
rGovemmentCodesections8l000-91014. 
CommissionregulationsappearatTitle2,sectionsl8l0g-
18997, ofthe California Code ofRegulations.
ffi


File No. 4-03-006
Page No. 2
3. 
Does Supervisor Shoemakerhave a conflict of interest disqualifring him
from making,particrpating in making or influencing a vote on the UVAP, based on his
economic interest in other clients within the TIVAP area who may be sources of income
equal to or greaterthan $500 within the last 12 months?
4. 
Does Supervisor Shoemaker have a conflict of interest disqualiffing him
from making, participatingin making or influencing a vote on the UVAP, based on his
economic interest in his business, Green Lion Landscape Services, which, among other
things, provides services to clients within the UVAP area?
CONCLUSIONS
1. 
No, unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the improvements to the access
road that would result from the development of the 80-acreparcel adjacent to his property
would have amaterial financial effect on the value of his properfy, sufficient to rebut the
presumption o f non-m ateiality .
2. 
Yes. The rural housing development corporation initiated aproceeding
before Supervisor Shoemaker's board and is a source of income in excess of $500 over
the past 12 months to him. Therefore, the corporation is directly involved in a
governmental decision before Supervisor Shoemaker's board, and his conflict of interest
is disqualiffing.
3. 
Possibly, depending on the applicable materiality standard.
4. 
No. Supervisor Shoemakerhas determined that the materiality standard
applicable to his business wjll not be met. Therefore, there is no disqualifyingconflict of
interest present related to Green Lion.
FACTS
The following facts were provided in your original letter and in subsequent
telephone conversations with Commission staffon March 10,2003.
Supervisor Richard Shoemaker represents the2n District of Mendocino County.
Supervisor Shoemaker has been a county supervisor for six years. IIis district is about
7.5 square miles in a counQr of approximately 3,500 square miles in size. Roughly 80%
of thepopulation of the 2"d district lives within the boundaries of the City of Uiciah. The
City of tlkiah is wholly contained in the 2"d district.
The county has undertaken the process of reviewing and possibly implementing
the Ukiah Valley Area Plan ("UVAP'). This plan, when and if adopted, will be a part of
the Mendocino County General Plan. The City of Ukiah is excluded from the UVAP
because the board of supervisors has no planning authority over the City of Ukiah.


FileNo. ,4.-03-006
Page No. 3
The UVAP is intended, when adopted, to provide comprehensive,long-term
policy direction for growth and development in the Ukiah Valley. The UVAP addresses
five of seven elements required by state law, excluding noise and housing. The three
major elements of the plan are:
Research Elements: Open space and conservation, historic and archaeological
safety and energy.
fnfrastructure Elements: Airport, community facilities and services, parks and
recreation, circulation and transportation.
Development Elements: Community design and land use.
Supervisor Shoemaker owns a 1.Zi-aqeparcel within the city limits of Ukiah. It
is his principal residence and the location of his landscape contracting business, He has
owned this property since 1990. His property lies approximately 550 feet from aportion
of the unincorporated county area included in the Ukiah Valley Area PIan.
There is an SO-acre parcel approximately 550 feet from Supervisor Shoemaker's
real property, which is currently undeveloped and is the subject of a possible zoning
designation change in the UVAP.
Supervisor Shoemaker's property is accessed by a substandard two-lane road at
this time. The road then continues through the SO-acre undeveloped parcel that would be
included in the UVAP. The city has plans to widen the road and connect it to a main east-
west street 1,100 feet to the east. The UVAP will propose the county cooperate and
pafiicipate in this upgrade, through financial and regulatory support. It will then be
designated as a main roadway connecting the only existing east-west corridor to a more
easterly, shorter but important east-west corridor. As development occurs, road
improvement will be required to occur on at least TOYo of the road Supervisor
Shoemaker's property faces. The 70oh of the road on which improvernent will be
required to occur will be the county portion of the road associated with the 80-acre
parcel; Supervisor Shoemaker's property is fronted by aportion of the road owned by the
city.
Supervisor Shoemaker also owns a landscape contracting company which is
operated as a sole,proprietorship. This business provides installation and landscape
maintenance services. The business, Green Lion Landscape Services ("Green Lion')
provides services to client properties within the UVAP area.
A client of Green Lion, which, as clarified in ourMarch l0,2003,telephone
conversation, is a source of income to Supervisor Shoemaker in excess of $500 over the
Tast 72 months, has an option agreernent to purchase real property in the UVAP are4
which the client wants redesignated and rezoned from agricultural to residential. This
option agreement has not yet been exercised. This client has also been in discussion with
landowners within 550 feet of Supervisor Shoemaker's real property to acquire that


File No. 4-03-006
Page No. 4
property. No agreernent has been entered into between these parties for that property.
This client is a rural housing development corporation, which acquires real property for
resale to low-income persons. This corporation also assists low-income persons to build
houses on this property. The property and houses are owned, ultimatelS by persons other
than the development corporation. Neighboring property to the property for which an
option to purchase exists, the Olga Hudson subdivision in the UVAP area, was aproperty
originally owned by the development corporation and is now owned by private residents.
Supervisor Shoemaker does not provide landscaping services to residents of this
subdivision, nor does Supervisor Shoemakerprovide services to properties owned by the
development corporation in the UVAP area.
Supervisor Shoemakerprovides landscape services to other clients with real
property ownership in the UVAP area, some of whom maybe sources of income equal to
or in excess of $500 over the past 12 months, according to facts obtained by telephone on
March 70,2003. With respect to these clients, Supervisor Shoemaker has represented
that no board of supervisor's decision involving action of the IIVAP, will result in an
increase or decrease in Green Lion's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; nor will the board's decision result in Green Lion incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; nor will the board's decision result in an increase or decrease
in the value of Green Lion's assets or liabilities of Green Lion by $20,000 ormore.
AITALYSIS
The primary purpose for the conflict of interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that "[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests ofpersons who have supported them." (Section S1001(b).) In furtherance of
this goal, section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating
in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under section 87100 requires
analysis of the following questions as outlined below.2
Step One: Is Supervisor Shoemaker a "public officialrr?
As a member of the Mendocino County Board of Supervisors, Supervisor
Shoemaker is a "mernber, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency" and, therefore, is subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act.
(Section 82048; regulation 1 8701 (a).)
' These questions are based on the Act's conflict-of-interest analysis provided at Regulation 18700(b).


File No. A-03-006
Page No. 5
step Two: rs supervisor shoemaker making, participating in making or
influencing a governmental decision?
As a member of the board of supervisors, Supervisor Shoemaker will "make a
governmental decision" if he votes on the UVAP. (Section 87l}l;regulations 18702 and
18702.1.)
Step Three: 'What are Supervisor Shoemaker's economic interests - 
the possible
sources of a conflict of interest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision'lf it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
materiaT financial effect, distingu'ishable from its effect on thepublic generally,on the
official, a member ofhis orher immediate family," or on any ofthe official's economic
interests, described as follows:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct orindirect investment 3 of $2,000 ormore (section 87103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, parhrer,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d);
regulation I 8703. 1(b));
a
a
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation
187O3.2);
A public official has an economic interest in.any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 ormore within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03 (c); regulation I 87 03 .3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(section 871 03(e); regulation 1870. .9;
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family .. this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 13703,5).
Supervisor Shoemaker has an economic interest in his personal residence and the
surrounding properfy, assuming he has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more
in the property and the strucfure. He has an economic interest in his business, Green Lion
3 An indirect investment or interest means aoy investrnent or interest owned by the spouse of an official or
by a mernber of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or
trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a
l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family" is defined at Section 820i9 as an official;s
spouse and dependent children.


File No. A-03-006
Page No. 6
Landscaping Services. Supervisor Shoemaker also has an economic interest in the
unnamed client that is a rural housing development corporation ("Development
Corporation') that seeks therezoning of theproperty within the UVAP areato which it
holds an option to purchase, as well as in any other clients who are sources of income
equal to or in excess of $500 for the 12 months preceding the decision.
Step f,'our: Are the economic interests of Supervisor Shoemaker directly or
indirectly involved in the goyernmental decisions?
Real Property
Real property is directly involved in a govemmental decision if anypart of that
real property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (orproposed boundaries) of the
real property which is the "subject ofthe govemmental decision.,a
Supervisor Shoemaker's property is located beyond 500 feet of the S0-acre parcel
that will be included within the UVAP, which presumptively makes the property
indirectly involved. Under ordinary circumstances, this would be the end of the analysis
as to whether Supervisor Shoemaker's property was directly or indirectly involved in the
govemmental decision. However, this is an unusual situation in which there is a
secondary decision imbedded within the larger decision that is even more significant to
the analysis than the larger decision.
In this case, the UVAP contains the proposal that the county "cooperate" with the
city in the improvement of the access road to Supervisor Shoemaker's residence, and
over the course of the development of the 80-acre parcel ofproperty adjacent to
Supervisor Shoemaker's land, approximately TOYo of the road Supervisor Shoemaker,s
property faces, although not the portion directly in front ofhis property, will undergo
improvement, and the road will be designated a main roadway, and connecting it to an
important east-west corridor.
Under these facts, because the county portion of the road that will be improved as
a result of development of the 80-acre parcel is the same 550 feet from his property as the
S0-acre parcel, itself, Supervisor Shoemaker's property is indirectly involved in this
decision, as we11.5
Regulation lSTW.2provides several other situations in which an official's real property is considered
"directly involved" in a governmental decision. (lS7M.2(a)(2)-(6).) None of these appear to appiy to Supervisor
Shoemaker's facts.
5 Regulation lS7}4.2(b)provides: "(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a) above, real property in which a
public official has an interest is not directly involved in a govemmental decision, but is indirecily involved if: . . . (2)
The decision solely concerns repairs, replacement, or maintenance of existing streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or
similar facilities." This regulation does not apply to determine that Supervisor Shoemaker's property is indirectl!
involved, because the decision at issue does not "solely concem repairs, replacement, or maintenance" of an existing
road. Rather, the road will be substantially improved as a result of the decision on the (IVAP, which will lead to
development ofthe SO-acre parcel ofproperty adjacent to Supervisor shoemaker's properly.


File No. 4-03-006
Page No. 7
Sources of Income and Supervisor Shoemakerrs Business
The same definition is applicable to sources of income and to business entities to
determine whether they are directly or indirectly involved in a governmental decision.
Set forth at regulation 18704.1(a)(1) and Q), it states:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person,
either directly or by an agent:
"(I) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject o{, the
proceeding conceming the decision before the official or
the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.,,
Based on this definition, the Development Corporation client is directly involved
in the governmental decision, because it has initiated a proceeding to have property
rezoned and redesignated from agricultural to residential. Any other sources of income to
Supervisor Shoemaker who fall within the definition listed above would,likewise, be
directly involved in a govemmental decision. For those sources ofincome.who have
neither initiated a proceeding nor are the subject of a proceeding, as defined above, those
persons are indirectly involved in the decision.
Likewise, Supervisor Shoemaker's business, Green Lion Landscape Services, has
initiated no proceeding and is not a named party in,nor is the subject of the proceeding
concerning the UVAP decision, according to the facts with which we have been
presented. Therefore, Green Lion is indirectly involved.
Steps Five and Six: What are the applicable materiality standards and is it
reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon
Supervisor Shoemaker's economic interests will meet these materiality standards?
Real Property
Materiality Standard: The financial effect of a govemmental decision on real
property which is indirectly involved in the goveflrmental decision is presumed not to be
material. (Regulatio n 187 05.2@)( 1 ).) That regulation continues:


File No. A-03-006
Page No. 8
"This presumptiolnfi:u.y be rebutted byproof thatthere are
specific circumstances regarding the governmental
decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic
interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have amateial financial efi[ect on the real
property in which the public official has an interest.
Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered
includg but are not limited to, circumstances where the
decision aflects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing
potential of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has
an economic interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy,
intensity ofuse, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits
of the neighborhood."
In considering these circumstances for rebuttal, it should be remembered that
material financial effect can mean either an increase or a decrease in property value or
enjoyment for the owner/occupant of the property.
In this case, there may be some issues related to the road improvements and
upgrades associated with the development of the S0-acre parcel adjacent to Supervisor
Shoemaker's property that may be sufficient to rebut the presumption of non-materiality.
We lack sufficient facts to analyze this issue further, but hightight the relevant regulations
for your review and analysis.
Sources of Income
A. 
Development Corporation: Because this source of income is directly
involved in a govemmental decision before supavisor Shoemaker,s board, the
materiality standard set forth at regulation 18705.3(a) applies:
"Directly involved sources of income. Any reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of
income to a public official, and who is directly involved in
a decision before the official's agency, is deemed material.,,
B. 
Indirectly fnvolved Sources of Ineome: Because these sources of
income are unidentified and unenumerated, this query having been added in ourMarch


File No. ,4.-03-006
Page No. 9
10,2003, telephone conversation, we can do no more than give a generaloverview of the
regulations for your information. The materiality standardsfor this category of economic
interest can be found at regulation 18705.3(b) (copy encloseo. For indiiecily involved
sources of income which are business entities, the materiality standards set forth in
regulation 18705.1(c). This regulation sets forth a series ofmateriality thresholds, based
on the size of the business entity. For indirectly involved sogrces ofincome which are
non-profit entities, including governmental entities, regulation 18705.3(b)(2)(A)-(F) sets
forth varying threshold amounts, depending on the entity's gross urr.rui i""iiptr and the
measurable financial effect of the decision on entity's income. For indirectly involved
sources of income who are individuals:
"(3) . . . The effect of a decision is material as to an
individual who is a source ofincome to an official if any of
the following applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual,s income,
investments or other tangible or intangible assets or
liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individua|s rear property
interest in a manner that is considered material unaer iitte
2, Califomia Code of Regulations, sections 1 g705.2(b).,,
Regulation 18705.2(b), referenced above, sets forth the materiality standard for a public
official's indirectly involved real property interests.
Supervisor Shoemaker,s Business Enti8
The materiality standards for an indirectly involved business entity in which a
public official has an economic interest are found at regulation 18705.1(c), which sets
forth varyrng materiality thresholds, based on the size of the business. dupervisor
Shoemaker has represented that "no board of supervisor's decision involving action of
the UVAP, will result in an increase or decrease in Green Lion's gross reveriues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more; nor will the board's decision result in
Green Lion incurring or avoiding additional expenses orreducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; nor will the board,s decision
result in an increase or decrease in the value of Green Lion's assets or liabilities of Green
Lion by $20,000 or more." Therefore, it appears that Supervisor Shoemaker has
reviewed regulation 18705.1(c), and determined that his business is not covered by
subdivisions (c)(1)-(3), and that Green Lion falls into subdivision (c)(4) of regulation
1 8705. 1, which provides:
"If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)(l)-
(3), the financial effect of a governmental decision on the
business entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable
that:


File No. 4-03-006
PageNo. l0
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 ormore; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity,s assets or
liabilities of $20,000 ormore."
Supervisor Shoemakerhas determined that this materiality standard will not be
met.
Reasonable Foreseeability: An effect upon economic interests is considered
"reasonably foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation
18706(a).) A financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreieeable,
but it must be more than a mere possibility. Qn re Thorner (1975) I FppC Ops. l9g.)
The determination of whether or not it is reasonably foreseeable that the
materiality standard will be met is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately for the
public official to decide.
Segmentation of Decision: Please note that for purposes of determining
conflicts of interest under the Act, governmental decisions are analyzed indepenJently to
determine if there will be a foreseeable and material financial efiteci on apublic official,s
economic interest. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops.77.) Therefore, under certain
circumstances, a public official disqualified from one decision may participate in other
related decisions, provided that the official's participation in the lattir does not affect the
decision in which he or she has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (SweeneyAdvice
Letter, No. A-89-639.) Some decisions are too interrelated to be considered sqlarately so
that the official's conflict of interest on one decision will be disqualifting for the other.
(Kilian Advice Letter, No. 4-89-522; Mtller Advice Letter, No. 4-82-11-9.) However, if
a governmental decision may be logically segregated, the public body maTrproceduraliy
segregate the decision prior to allowing the public official to participate.u -We 
do not
6 The Commission has previously advised public officials that the procedural segregation must include the
following three steps: (1) the decision in which the public official has a disqualifying fina-nci-al interest should be
segregated from the other decisions on the public body's agenda; (2) the decision-should be considered first and a final
decision should be reached without the disqualified official's participation; and (3) once a final decision has been
reached, the disqualified ofificial may participate in the other decisions so tong as fils or her participation does not result
in re-opening the previous decision, or in any other way affect the decision from which rtre ofnciaiwas disqualified.
(Ennis Adice Letter, No. A-94-203.)


File No. 4-03-006
Page No. 11
have enough information regarding the decisions before Supervisor Shoemaker to
determine whether the segmentation procedure will be necessary or useful to him.
Steps Seven and Eight: 66Public Generally" and "Legally Required participation,
Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (Section 83ll2;regulation 18707.) Stei-eight is an
excqrtion that applies when the official's participation is legally requirea.lSection
87101; regulation 18708.) Both of these narrowly construed eiceptions are fact-driven,
and nothing in the facts you have provided would indicate that either of these exceptions
would apply to Supervisor Shoemaker's situafion. Accordingly, we have not analyzed
these excqrtions in relation to the facts you provided
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
SincerelS
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
/
By:
B. Armskong
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
HBAjg

A client of Green Lion, which, as clarified in ourMarch l0,2003,telephone
conversation, is a source of income to Supervisor Shoemaker in excess of $500 over the
Tast 72 months, has an option agreernent to purchase real property in the UVAP are4
which the client wants redesignated and rezoned from agricultural to residential. This
option agreement has not yet been exercised. This client has also been in discussion with
landowners within 550 feet of Supervisor Shoemaker's real property to acquire that
property. No agreernent has been entered into between these parties for that property.
This client is a rural housing development corporation, which acquires real property for
resale to low-income persons. This corporation also assists low-income persons to build
houses on this property. The property and houses are owned, ultimatelS by persons other
than the development corporation. Neighboring property to the property for which an
option to purchase exists, the Olga Hudson subdivision in the UVAP area, was aproperty
originally owned by the development corporation and is now owned by private residents.
Supervisor Shoemaker does not provide landscaping services to residents of this
subdivision, nor does Supervisor Shoemakerprovide services to properties owned by the
development corporation in the UVAP area.
Supervisor Shoemakerprovides landscape services to other clients with real
property ownership in the UVAP area, some of whom maybe sources of income equal to
or in excess of $500 over the past 12 months, according to facts obtained by telephone on
March 70,2003. With respect to these clients, Supervisor Shoemaker has represented
that no board of supervisor's decision involving action of the IIVAP, will result in an
increase or decrease in Green Lion's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; nor will the board's decision result in Green Lion incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; nor will the board's decision result in an increase or decrease
in the value of Green Lion's assets or liabilities of Green Lion by $20,000 ormore.
AITALYSIS
The primary purpose for the conflict of interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that "[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests ofpersons who have supported them." (Section S1001(b).) In furtherance of
this goal, section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating
in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under section 87100 requires
sections.analysis:
of the following questions as outlined below.2
Step One: Is Supervisor Shoemaker a "public officialrr?
As a member of the Mendocino County Board of Supervisors, Supervisor
Shoemaker is a "mernber, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency" and, therefore, is subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act.
(Section 82048; regulation 1 8701 (a).)
' These questions are based on the Act's conflict-of-interest analysis provided at Regulation 18700(b).
step Two: rs supervisor shoemaker making, participating in making or
influencing a governmental decision?
As a member of the board of supervisors, Supervisor Shoemaker will "make a
governmental decision" if he votes on the UVAP. (Section 87l}l;regulations 18702 and
18702.1.)
Step Three: 'What are Supervisor Shoemaker's economic interests - 
the possible
sources of a conflict of interest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision'lf it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
materiaT financial effect, distingu'ishable from its effect on thepublic generally,on the
official, a member ofhis orher immediate family," or on any ofthe official's economic
interests, described as follows:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct orindirect investment 3 of $2,000 ormore (section 87103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, parhrer,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d);
regulation I 8703. 1(b));
a
a
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation
187O3.2);
A public official has an economic interest in.any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 ormore within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03 (c); regulation I 87 03 .3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(section 871 03(e); regulation 1870. .9;
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family .. this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 13703,5).
Supervisor Shoemaker has an economic interest in his personal residence and the
surrounding properfy, assuming he has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more
in the property and the strucfure. He has an economic interest in his business, Green Lion
3 An indirect investment or interest means aoy investrnent or interest owned by the spouse of an official or
by a mernber of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or
trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a
l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family" is defined at Section 820i9 as an official;s
spouse and dependent children.
Landscaping Services. Supervisor Shoemaker also has an economic interest in the
unnamed client that is a rural housing development corporation ("Development
Corporation') that seeks therezoning of theproperty within the UVAP areato which it
holds an option to purchase, as well as in any other clients who are sources of income
equal to or in excess of $500 for the 12 months preceding the decision.
Step f,'our: Are the economic interests of Supervisor Shoemaker directly or
indirectly involved in the goyernmental decisions?
Real Property
Real property is directly involved in a govemmental decision if anypart of that
real property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (orproposed boundaries) of the
real property which is the "subject ofthe govemmental decision.,a
Supervisor Shoemaker's property is located beyond 500 feet of the S0-acre parcel
that will be included within the UVAP, which presumptively makes the property
indirectly involved. Under ordinary circumstances, this would be the end of the analysis
as to whether Supervisor Shoemaker's property was directly or indirectly involved in the
govemmental decision. However, this is an unusual situation in which there is a
secondary decision imbedded within the larger decision that is even more significant to
the analysis than the larger decision.
In this case, the UVAP contains the proposal that the county "cooperate" with the
city in the improvement of the access road to Supervisor Shoemaker's residence, and
over the course of the development of the 80-acre parcel ofproperty adjacent to
Supervisor Shoemaker's land, approximately TOYo of the road Supervisor Shoemaker,s
property faces, although not the portion directly in front ofhis property, will undergo
improvement, and the road will be designated a main roadway, and connecting it to an
important east-west corridor.
Under these facts, because the county portion of the road that will be improved as
a result of development of the 80-acre parcel is the same 550 feet from his property as the
S0-acre parcel, itself, Supervisor Shoemaker's property is indirectly involved in this
decision, as we11.5
Regulation lSTW.2provides several other situations in which an official's real property is considered
"directly involved" in a governmental decision. (lS7M.2(a)(2)-(6).) None of these appear to appiy to Supervisor
Shoemaker's facts.
5 Regulation lS7}4.2(b)provides: "(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a) above, real property in which a
public official has an interest is not directly involved in a govemmental decision, but is indirecily involved if: . . . (2)
The decision solely concerns repairs, replacement, or maintenance of existing streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or
similar facilities." This regulation does not apply to determine that Supervisor Shoemaker's property is indirectl!
involved, because the decision at issue does not "solely concem repairs, replacement, or maintenance" of an existing
road. Rather, the road will be substantially improved as a result of the decision on the (IVAP, which will lead to
development ofthe SO-acre parcel ofproperty adjacent to Supervisor shoemaker's properly.
Sources of Income and Supervisor Shoemakerrs Business
The same definition is applicable to sources of income and to business entities to
determine whether they are directly or indirectly involved in a governmental decision.
Set forth at regulation 18704.1(a)(1) and Q), it states:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person,
either directly or by an agent:
"(I) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject o{, the
proceeding conceming the decision before the official or
the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.,,
Based on this definition, the Development Corporation client is directly involved
in the governmental decision, because it has initiated a proceeding to have property
rezoned and redesignated from agricultural to residential. Any other sources of income to
Supervisor Shoemaker who fall within the definition listed above would,likewise, be
directly involved in a govemmental decision. For those sources ofincome.who have
neither initiated a proceeding nor are the subject of a proceeding, as defined above, those
persons are indirectly involved in the decision.
Likewise, Supervisor Shoemaker's business, Green Lion Landscape Services, has
initiated no proceeding and is not a named party in,nor is the subject of the proceeding
concerning the UVAP decision, according to the facts with which we have been
presented. Therefore, Green Lion is indirectly involved.
Steps Five and Six: What are the applicable materiality standards and is it
reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon
Supervisor Shoemaker's economic interests will meet these materiality standards?
Real Property
Materiality Standard: The financial effect of a govemmental decision on real
property which is indirectly involved in the goveflrmental decision is presumed not to be
material. (Regulatio n 187 05.2@)( 1 ).) That regulation continues:
"This presumptiolnfi:u.y be rebutted byproof thatthere are
specific circumstances regarding the governmental
decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic
interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have amateial financial efi[ect on the real
property in which the public official has an interest.
Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered
includg but are not limited to, circumstances where the
decision aflects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing
potential of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has
an economic interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy,
intensity ofuse, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits
of the neighborhood."
In considering these circumstances for rebuttal, it should be remembered that
material financial effect can mean either an increase or a decrease in property value or
enjoyment for the owner/occupant of the property.
In this case, there may be some issues related to the road improvements and
upgrades associated with the development of the S0-acre parcel adjacent to Supervisor
Shoemaker's property that may be sufficient to rebut the presumption of non-materiality.
We lack sufficient facts to analyze this issue further, but hightight the relevant regulations
for your review and analysis.
Sources of Income
A. 
Development Corporation: Because this source of income is directly
involved in a govemmental decision before supavisor Shoemaker,s board, the
materiality standard set forth at regulation 18705.3(a) applies:
"Directly involved sources of income. Any reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of
income to a public official, and who is directly involved in
a decision before the official's agency, is deemed material.,,
B. 
Indirectly fnvolved Sources of Ineome: Because these sources of
income are unidentified and unenumerated, this query having been added in ourMarch


File No. ,4.-03-006
10,2003, telephone conversation, we can do no more than give a generaloverview of the
regulations for your information. The materiality standardsfor this category of economic
interest can be found at regulation 18705.3(b) (copy encloseo. For indiiecily involved
sources of income which are business entities, the materiality standards set forth in
regulation 18705.1(c). This regulation sets forth a series ofmateriality thresholds, based
on the size of the business entity. For indirectly involved sogrces ofincome which are
non-profit entities, including governmental entities, regulation 18705.3(b)(2)(A)-(F) sets
forth varying threshold amounts, depending on the entity's gross urr.rui i""iiptr and the
measurable financial effect of the decision on entity's income. For indirectly involved
sources of income who are individuals:
"(3) . . . The effect of a decision is material as to an
individual who is a source ofincome to an official if any of
the following applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual,s income,
investments or other tangible or intangible assets or
liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individua|s rear property
interest in a manner that is considered material unaer iitte
2, Califomia Code of Regulations, sections 1 g705.2(b).,,
Regulation 18705.2(b), referenced above, sets forth the materiality standard for a public
official's indirectly involved real property interests.
Supervisor Shoemaker,s Business Enti8
The materiality standards for an indirectly involved business entity in which a
public official has an economic interest are found at regulation 18705.1(c), which sets
forth varyrng materiality thresholds, based on the size of the business. dupervisor
Shoemaker has represented that "no board of supervisor's decision involving action of
the UVAP, will result in an increase or decrease in Green Lion's gross reveriues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more; nor will the board's decision result in
Green Lion incurring or avoiding additional expenses orreducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; nor will the board,s decision
result in an increase or decrease in the value of Green Lion's assets or liabilities of Green
Lion by $20,000 or more." Therefore, it appears that Supervisor Shoemaker has
reviewed regulation 18705.1(c), and determined that his business is not covered by
subdivisions (c)(1)-(3), and that Green Lion falls into subdivision (c)(4) of regulation
1 8705. 1, which provides:
"If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)(l)-
(3), the financial effect of a governmental decision on the
business entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable
that:


PageNo. l0
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 ormore; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity,s assets or
liabilities of $20,000 ormore."
Supervisor Shoemakerhas determined that this materiality standard will not be
met.
Reasonable Foreseeability: An effect upon economic interests is considered
"reasonably foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation
18706(a).) A financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreieeable,
but it must be more than a mere possibility. Qn re Thorner (1975) I FppC Ops. l9g.)
The determination of whether or not it is reasonably foreseeable that the
materiality standard will be met is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately for the
public official to decide.
Segmentation of Decision: Please note that for purposes of determining
conflicts of interest under the Act, governmental decisions are analyzed indepenJently to
determine if there will be a foreseeable and material financial efiteci on apublic official,s
economic interest. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops.77.) Therefore, under certain
circumstances, a public official disqualified from one decision may participate in other
related decisions, provided that the official's participation in the lattir does not affect the
decision in which he or she has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (SweeneyAdvice
Letter, No. A-89-639.) Some decisions are too interrelated to be considered sqlarately so
that the official's conflict of interest on one decision will be disqualifting for the other.
(Kilian Advice Letter, No. 4-89-522; Mtller Advice Letter, No. 4-82-11-9.) However, if
a governmental decision may be logically segregated, the public body maTrproceduraliy
segregate the decision prior to allowing the public official to participate.u -We 
do not
6 The Commission has previously advised public officials that the procedural segregation must include the
following three steps: (1) the decision in which the public official has a disqualifying fina-nci-al interest should be
segregated from the other decisions on the public body's agenda; (2) the decision-should be considered first and a final
decision should be reached without the disqualified official's participation; and (3) once a final decision has been
reached, the disqualified ofificial may participate in the other decisions so tong as fils or her participation does not result
in re-opening the previous decision, or in any other way affect the decision from which rtre ofnciaiwas disqualified.
(Ennis Adice Letter, No. A-94-203.)
have enough information regarding the decisions before Supervisor Shoemaker to
determine whether the segmentation procedure will be necessary or useful to him.
Steps Seven and Eight: 66Public Generally" and "Legally Required participation,
Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (Section 83ll2;regulation 18707.) Stei-eight is an
excqrtion that applies when the official's participation is legally requirea.lSection
87101; regulation 18708.) Both of these narrowly construed eiceptions are fact-driven,
and nothing in the facts you have provided would indicate that either of these exceptions
would apply to Supervisor Shoemaker's situafion. Accordingly, we have not analyzed
these excqrtions in relation to the facts you provided

QUESTION: l. 
Does Supervisor Shoemaker have a conflict of interest disqualifring him
from making,particrpating in making or influencing a vote on the Ukiah Valley Area
Plan ("UVAP'), based on his economic interest in his personal residence and the
improvements to the access road to his residence that would accompany approval ofthe
UVAP?
2. 
Does Supervisor Shoemaker have a conflict of interest disqualifring him
from making, particrpating in making or influencing a vote on the UVAP, based o, his
economic interest in a client who is a source of income to him in excess of $500 over the
past 12 months, and who has an option to purchase properfy within the UVAP area and
seeks rezoning of that property from agricultural to residential for the building of low-
incorne housing, or from the portion of the TIVAP dealing with that decision, if that
decision is segregated from the rest of the UVAP?
rGovemmentCodesections8l000-91014. 
CommissionregulationsappearatTitle2,sectionsl8l0g-
18997, ofthe California Code ofRegulations.
ffi
3. 
Does Supervisor Shoemakerhave a conflict of interest disqualifring him
from making,particrpating in making or influencing a vote on the UVAP, based on his
economic interest in other clients within the TIVAP area who may be sources of income
equal to or greaterthan $500 within the last 12 months?
4. 
Does Supervisor Shoemaker have a conflict of interest disqualiffing him
from making, participatingin making or influencing a vote on the UVAP, based on his
economic interest in his business, Green Lion Landscape Services, which, among other
things, provides services to clients within the UVAP area?

CONCLUSION: 1. 
No, unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the improvements to the access
road that would result from the development of the 80-acreparcel adjacent to his property
would have amaterial financial effect on the value of his properfy, sufficient to rebut the
presumption o f non-m ateiality .
2. 
Yes. The rural housing development corporation initiated aproceeding
before Supervisor Shoemaker's board and is a source of income in excess of $500 over
the past 12 months to him. Therefore, the corporation is directly involved in a
governmental decision before Supervisor Shoemaker's board, and his conflict of interest
is disqualiffing.
3. 
Possibly, depending on the applicable materiality standard.
4. 
No. Supervisor Shoemakerhas determined that the materiality standard
applicable to his business wjll not be met. Therefore, there is no disqualifyingconflict of
interest present related to Green Lion.

Does Supervisor Shoemaker have a conflict of interest disqualifring him from making,particrpating in making or influencing a vote on the Ukiah Valley Area Plan ("UVAP'), based on his economic interest in his personal residence and the improvements to the access road to his residence that would accompany approval ofthe UVAP? 2. Does Supervisor Shoemaker have a conflict of interest disqualifring him from making, particrpating in making or influencing a vote on the UVAP, based o, his economic interest in a client who is a source of income to him in excess of $500 over the past 12 months, and who has an option to purchase properfy within the UVAP area and seeks rezoning of that property from agricultural to residential for the building of low- incorne housing, or from the portion of the TIVAP dealing with that decision, if that decision is segregated from the rest of the UVAP? rGovemmentCodesections8l000-91014. CommissionregulationsappearatTitle2,sectionsl8l0g- 18997, ofthe California Code ofRegulations. ffi File No. 4-03-006 Page No. 2 3. Does Supervisor Shoemakerhave a conflict of interest disqualifring him from making,particrpating in making or influencing a vote on the UVAP, based on his economic interest in other clients within the TIVAP area who may be sources of income equal to or greaterthan $500 within the last 12 months? 4. Does Supervisor Shoemaker have a conflict of interest disqualiffing him from making, participatingin making or influencing a vote on the UVAP, based on his economic interest in his business, Green Lion Landscape Services, which, among other things, provides services to clients within the UVAP area? No, unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the improvements to the access road that would result from the development of the 80-acreparcel adjacent to his property would have amaterial financial effect on the value of his properfy, sufficient to rebut the presumption o f non-m ateiality . 2. Yes. The rural housing development corporation initiated aproceeding before Supervisor Shoemaker's board and is a source of income in excess of $500 over the past 12 months to him. Therefore, the corporation is directly involved in a governmental decision before Supervisor Shoemaker's board, and his conflict of interest is disqualiffing. 3. Possibly, depending on the applicable materiality standard. 4. No. Supervisor Shoemakerhas determined that the materiality standard applicable to his business wjll not be met. Therefore, there is no disqualifyingconflict of interest present related to Green Lion. FACTS The following facts were provided in your original letter and in subsequent
